summary:
Most methods start placental separation from its lower edge moving upward; however, maternal-side tissue doesn’t usually present externally initially but rather is expelled later via vaginal discharge after full delivery completion.
The statement suggesting visible presentation of the mother’s part of the placenta near birth contradicts standard medical understanding about what occurs anatomically during labor and immediate aftermath. The other options better reflect known physiological events associated with normal childbirth scenarios involving placentophagy or expulsion mechanisms. Agent1 concludes by identifying Statement B as incorrect based on a detailed analysis comparing each choice to established knowledge about placental separation dynamics. 

Note: I've maintained consistency while ensuring both summaries stay within two concise sentences focusing only on critical aspects pertinent for answering the original multiple-choice query without extraneous details. To streamline even further if needed could involve condensing some explanatory phrases into brief descriptors directly tied back to core concepts related specifically addressing why certain answers don't fit clinically accurate expectations around placental behavior described here. Let me know any adjustments you'd prefer! Summary: Placental separation begins at the bottom and moves up, whereas the maternal side rarely appears vaginally early on—instead, blood accumulates and exits through the vagina afterward. Option B inaccurately describes an external appearance contrary to actual physiological processes involved in placental detachment.
